assumptions: The intragroup dividend has already been recognised as an intercompany receivable/payable at reporting date.
The relevant issue is hedge accounting in consolidated financial statements under IFRS 9 for foreign exchange risk only.
The functional currencies of the group entities may differ; otherwise the foreign exchange exception for intragroup items may not be relevant.
No facts are provided on the hedging instrument, hedge timing, or whether foreign exchange differences survive consolidation.

approaches.label: Fair value hedge of the recognised intragroup receivable
approaches.reasoning: A recognised asset or liability can be a hedged item in a fair value hedge. In consolidated statements, an intragroup monetary item qualifies only if its foreign exchange gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation.
conditions: The dividend receivable/payable is a recognised monetary item
Foreign exchange gains or losses on the intragroup item are not fully eliminated on consolidation
Only the foreign currency risk component is designated if separately identifiable and reliably measurable
Formal designation, documentation and hedge effectiveness requirements are met at inception
approaches.practical_implication: Document the hedge at consolidated level on the residual foreign exchange exposure that survives elimination.

source_contributions.summary: A recognised asset or liability can be a hedged item, and a fair value hedge covers changes in fair value attributable to a particular risk.
An intragroup monetary item may qualify in consolidated financial statements if it creates foreign exchange gains or losses that are not fully eliminated.
Risk component designation, reliable measurement, formal documentation and hedge effectiveness criteria must be satisfied.

approaches.label: Cash flow hedge of a forecast intragroup dividend
approaches.reasoning: A highly probable forecast intragroup transaction may qualify in consolidated statements if it is in a foreign currency and the foreign exchange risk will affect consolidated profit or loss. This is mainly a pre-recognition alternative, not the treatment of an already booked receivable.
conditions: The hedge is designated while the dividend is still a highly probable forecast transaction
The transaction is denominated in a currency other than the functional currency of the entity entering into it
The foreign exchange risk will affect consolidated profit or loss
The forecast transaction is highly probable and reliably measurable
Formal designation, documentation and hedge effectiveness requirements are met at inception
approaches.practical_implication: Use this only if hedge documentation is established before the dividend becomes a recognised receivable.

source_contributions.summary: A cash flow hedge covers variability in cash flows of a recognised item or a highly probable forecast transaction that could affect profit or loss.
A highly probable forecast intragroup transaction may qualify in consolidated statements only if the currency and profit-or-loss conditions are met.
The forecast transaction must be highly probable, and IFRS 9 documentation and hedge effectiveness criteria still apply.

approaches.label: No hedge accounting if the intragroup FX exposure is fully eliminated
approaches.reasoning: Hedged items are generally limited to exposures with parties external to the reporting entity. If the intragroup receivable/payable does not leave residual foreign exchange gains or losses in consolidation, the intragroup exception is not available.
conditions: Foreign exchange gains or losses on the intragroup item are fully eliminated on consolidation
The item is not being designated under a separate qualifying hedge framework such as a net investment hedge
approaches.practical_implication: Do not apply consolidated hedge documentation to the dividend receivable itself if no residual consolidated foreign exchange exposure remains.

source_contributions.summary: For hedge accounting, hedged items normally must be with a party external to the reporting entity.
The only relevant intragroup exception in the context provided is where foreign exchange gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge as an alternative framework
approaches.reasoning: A separate hedge accounting model exists for foreign exchange risk on a net investment in a foreign operation. It applies to the net investment exposure, not to the intragroup dividend receivable itself, and can qualify only once in consolidated financial statements.
conditions: The exposure being hedged is a qualifying net investment in a foreign operation included in the consolidated financial statements
The hedged amount does not exceed the carrying amount of the foreign operation's net assets
The hedged risk is between the functional currency of the foreign operation and the functional currency of a parent entity
Formal designation, documentation and hedge effectiveness requirements are met
The same net investment foreign exchange exposure is not designated more than once in the ultimate parent's consolidated financial statements
approaches.practical_implication: If the real objective is to hedge the foreign operation rather than the dividend receivable, document a net investment hedge instead.

source_contributions.summary: A hedge of a net investment in a foreign operation is one of the three hedge accounting models, subject to IFRS 9 designation and effectiveness rules.
The hedged item is an amount of net assets in a foreign operation, and the hedged risk is the foreign exchange exposure between the foreign operation and a parent's functional currency.
The hedging instrument may be held anywhere in the group, but the same net investment foreign exchange exposure can qualify only once in consolidated financial statements.
